full-bleed.content: 2024  •  Oman; Jebel Akhdar and desert landscapes

text-first.content: Omani Landscapes began at the dawn of generative AI, asking a single question: what is a photograph when an image can be spun up from a prompt? Each work starts as a photograph Lisa made in Oman with a Leica, then strips that image down through software until only its underlying patterns remain — and rebuilds those patterns into something new that no longer looks anything like a photograph. The result is a body of work that is genuinely photographic in origin yet reads as pure abstraction. Where generative AI can now produce images that look like photographs but are not, these are photographs that do not look like photographs — posing the question at the center of the project: is a photograph defined by form (what it looks like) or by function (how it was made)?
2024; Oman (Leica source photographs); software pattern-extraction then reconstruction; made at the dawn of generative AI; interrogates form vs function in photography

specifications.content: Oman; Jebel Akhdar and desert landscapes
Archival pigment print on Hahnemühle bright white baryta

text-first.content:
Winterblue was born from a winter search for Lisa's missing family dog. For 11 days she and her community searched. Sleeping in her car, searching in whiteouts, she met the full force of winter in the form of fear, isolation, temperature, and overwhelming discomfort. The dog was found unharmed.

Winterblue I is presented as a large-scale contact sheet — a grid of 125 individual photographs, one set of frames for each day spent on the Camas Prairie. The project carries regional character of embracing discomfort into winter atmosphere and emotional terrain.

The work demonstrates the practice — entering difficulty deliberately, staying long enough for perception to shift, and returning with images shaped by that exposure.
2023; Camas Prairie, Idaho; 17 trips January-April under sub-zero conditions; large-scale contact sheet of 125 photographs

AI, robotics, and accelerated automation are redrawing social order. Blue-collar work gains status and leverage while routine white-collar roles compress. The iceberg becomes a clean grammar for that shift. Traditional ice shows white above and deep…

text-first.content:
A minimalist photographic work that renders a flipped iceberg as the visible inversion of class power: blue rises, white sinks.

AI, robotics, and accelerated automation are redrawing social order. Blue-collar work gains status and leverage while routine white-collar roles compress. The iceberg becomes a clean grammar for that shift. Traditional ice shows white above and deep blue below. A flipped iceberg reverses those planes. The surface turns blue. White drops under the line.

FLIPPED reads the present and projects the near future: AI and automation shift power from routine white-collar administration to blue work—build, repair, operate. LISA WOOD STUDIO grounds the argument in two sources: medium-format photographs from two Greenland expeditions and AI images authored through precise prompts. The pair creates a single visual grammar: a flipped iceberg where deep blue rises above the line and white drops below it. Ice and light carry the claim without rhetoric. The sequence lands the inversion in one clean beat and holds on the new order.

specifications.content: Archival pigment print on Hahnemühle bright white baryta

text-first.content: Palouse is a photographic study of the farmed landscape of the Palouse region of the inland Pacific Northwest, where wind-deposited loess has built a terrain of deep, rolling hills given almost entirely to dryland agriculture. The work reads the cultivated surface as a record of both elemental force and human labor — contour-plowed slopes, seasonal color, and the long geometry of wheat, barley, and legume fields shaped to the undulation of the land.

shot circa 2013 (artist recollection); Nikon D800; Palouse region; rolling loess hills up to ~500 ft over Columbia River Basalt; wind-deposited Pleistocene glacial silt; one of the most productive non-irrigated (dryland) farming regions in the world; largest U.S. lentil-growing region

text-first.content:
SIDE EFFECTS introduces a prescribing cascade in which one drug is introduced to treat the side effect of another drug, connecting 4 prescription drugs.
The drugs profiled within the cascade are imaginary; their listed side effects are not. Each side effect is approved for one or more prescription drugs in the US by the United States Food and Drug Administration.
SIDE EFFECTS looks at the FDA and Big Pharma’s role in perpetuating prescribing cascades in a society molded by increased expectations and diminished timelines.
Rather than invest in making current drugs safer let us regard side effects as symptoms and sell and consume new drugs to treat them.
SIDE EFFECTS is not a negation of the pharmaceutical industry; it's a push for increased responsibility.

text-first.content:
We have limitless insight into the daily interests and activities of friends, acquaintances, and strangers alike thanks to the vast reach of social media. Our thoughts, interests, and attention form the coveted treasure at the end of the advertisers' rainbow.
Yet, when viewed in  the context of global events shaping the 21st century, how well do we understand the thoughts of others? ATTENTION chronicles the most popular search queries annually, starting from 2001, the first year of available data, through 2021.
In hindsight of significant national and global events, ATTENTION represents 20 years of allegorical insight into what's been on our minds.
The five-minute recording illuminates each of the twenty year's most popular keywords globally as well as the most common "how-to" and "what-is" queries within the United States.
The compilation reflects Google's advancement in data analytics over 2 decades: in 2001, Google released top keyword search data worldwide. Beginning in 2006, the company created Google Trends, introducing numerous sub-search categories such as top people search, news & entertainment stories, top searches by region & country, and others.

where fortune & fortunes are lost and found
to the place that 'Falls the Shadow'

text-first.content:
Luxuriate in discomfort is a central philosophy for Lisa Wood and a theme running through her practice — stepping into challenging conditions and staying there long enough for perception to change. It is a studio philosophy carried across three projects: a book, a one-night installation, and a public art project concept to improve teenage mental health.

Luxuriate in Discomfort considers our relationship with discomfort, and how the relationship shapes our experience. Together, the works confront our pursuit of comfort and luxury via a double entendre: by living a life of luxury in day-to-day torment, and by seeking discomfort as a means toward contentment. Born out of a 2.5 year inquiry into the nature of pain, the work asks what is luxury's ultimate prize, is it freedom?
2020–2024; eight-year personal discomfort practice; three projects — book (2020), one-night installation (2020), LUX public art concept (2024); modalities: sauna, cold plunge, whole-body vibration

text-first.content:
Luxuriate In Discomfort is a 30-page digest that explores how regular engagement with discomfort can lead to long-term contentment, written by the artist in 2020 for her eldest son as he begins to navigate the world post college, graduating into a pandemic-induced lockdown. It is a compilation of thoughts and ideas that emerged during the artist's water sessions over the past 2.5 years.

The book is written one reflective line per page, building from "cultivating a thirst for discomfort cultivates the way to personal fulfillment" toward the closing line, "ask a lot of yourself."
2020; 30-page digest; 7 inches square; one reflective line per page; written for the artist's eldest son

full-bleed.content: Luxuriate in Discomfort: December, Sun Valley

text-first.content: Luxuriate in Discomfort: December, Sun Valley

Luxuriating In Discomfort is a group of works born out of a 2.5 year inquiry into the nature of sadness and pain. Comprised of poetry, a book, a large painting, and a video clip, the work is autobiographical in nature, and serves as the culmination of a period of grief and transformation that began on July 1, 2018, the day I resolved to approach my own self-care with sincerity.

From use of the bedroom as installation space to employing a lullaby palette, Luxuriating In Discomfort dwells in double entendres, touching on both the multi-dimensionality and the potentiality of our experience. Water emerges as a primary theme, as both subject matter in the poems and as visual metaphor in the painting, memorializing the elemental medium that opened the pathway towards transformation.

A one-night-only exhibition staged inside the artist's bedroom in Sun Valley, Idaho in December 2020 at the height of the pandemic, opened to a small masked, distanced audience. Works on view: a mixed media painting, poetry (HEAT and COLD), the book Luxuriate in Discomfort, and a video clip (Hedge Crawl, from Andy Goldsworthy's Leaning into the Wind).
December 2020; Sun Valley, Idaho; one-night-only bedroom installation; works: mixed media painting (begun as a year painting January 1, 2019), HEAT & COLD poems, book, video

text-first.content:
LUX is a public art installation concept that addresses the teenage mental health crisis through an intentional discomfort practice: cold plunge, sauna, and whole-body vibration. Built on the artist's own eight-year practice, LUX offers young adults a constructive, approachable framework for building a working relationship with adversity — pairing physical rejuvenation with the psychological reward that follows.

LUX is a metaphorical satire of the LAX sign found at the entrance to Los Angeles International Airport, and references Southern California's historical and current place as a global trendsetter in what it means to live an extraordinarily comfortable life. HEAT and COLD symbolize oppression and rejection personified by excessively hot and cold environments and present an alternate path toward achieving comfort and freedom. Two vending machines dispense the book Luxuriate in Discomfort.

The project is structured around three movements — Paradox, Practice, and Gesture — and grew from the artist's personal connection to the young adult mental health crisis.
2024; public art concept; teenage mental health; three modalities — sauna, cold plunge, whole-body vibration; structured as Paradox / Practice / Gesture
